summary: Graham argues that the best way to use college is to get very good at programming by working hard on engaging, self-driven projects and by learning from smart peers and professors. He downplays the need for heavy math for most hacking, while still valuing math for its conceptual power, and advises focusing on fields and problems with real intellectual substance rather than fashionable or bogus ones. For jobs and grad school, he emphasizes aiming high, building real projects, and following what you genuinely like because faking stops working after college.
key_points: Becoming a great hacker comes from sustained work on hard, self-chosen problems and learning from the smartest communities around you.
Math isn’t strictly necessary for most hacking, but studying it deeply pays off as a source of powerful metaphors.
Many academic fields are less useful because they chase novelty or fashion rather than hard, honest problems.
College should stretch you beyond job requirements; real-world programming is larger, messier, and requires problem discovery.
Grad school is worthwhile if you want research, but it’s often uncomfortable and dissertation-heavy; genuine interest matters most.
money_quotes: The way to be good at programming is to work (a) a lot (b) on hard problems.
In your own projects you don't have to worry about novelty (as professors do) or profitability (as businesses do).
One of the most valuable things you could do in college would be to learn what math is really about.

The programs you write in classes differ in three critical ways from the ones you'll write in the real world: they're small; you get to start from scratch; and the problem is usually artificial and predetermined.
So the best thing you can do in college, whether you want to get into grad school or just be good at hacking, is figure out what you truly like.
key_insight: Use college to pursue hard, self-motivated work and build real projects while following what you genuinely enjoy, because that’s what turns you into a strong hacker and makes later paths—jobs or grad school—actually work.
